{{Short description|Branch of physics studying electromagnetic phenomena before quantum theory}}
{{Infobox physics
| name = Classical electromagnetism
| image = 
| caption = 
| topics = [[Electromagnetism]], [[Maxwell's equations]], [[Lorentz force]], [[Electric field]], [[Magnetic field]]
}}

'''Classical electromagnetism''' or '''classical electrodynamics''' is a branch of theoretical [[physics]] that studies the interactions between [[electric charge]]s and [[electric current|currents]] using an extension of the [[Classical mechanics|classical Newtonian model]]. The theory provides a description of [[electromagnetic phenomena]] whenever the relevant [[length]] scales and field strengths are large enough that [[quantum mechanics|quantum mechanical]] effects are negligible.

== Fundamental equations ==

=== Maxwell's equations ===
{{Main|Maxwell's equations}}
The complete description of classical electromagnetism is given by [[Maxwell's equations]]:
:<math>\nabla \cdot \mathbf{E} = \frac{\rho}{\varepsilon_0}</math> ([[Gauss's law]])
:<math>\nabla \cdot \mathbf{B} = 0</math> ([[Gauss's law for magnetism]])
:<math>\nabla \times \mathbf{E} = -\frac{\partial \mathbf{B}}{\partial t}</math> ([[Faraday's law of induction]])
:<math>\nabla \times \mathbf{B} = \mu_0 \mathbf{J} + \mu_0\varepsilon_0 \frac{\partial \mathbf{E}}{\partial t}</math> ([[Ampère's circuital law]] with Maxwell's correction)

=== Lorentz force ===
{{Main|Lorentz force}}
The force on a charged particle in electromagnetic fields is:
:<math>\mathbf{F} = q(\mathbf{E} + \mathbf{v} \times \mathbf{B})</math>

== Key phenomena ==
* '''[[Electrostatics]]''' — forces between stationary charges; described by [[Coulomb's law]]
* '''[[Magnetostatics]]''' — currents producing steady magnetic fields; [[Biot–Savart law]], [[Ampère's law]]
* '''[[Electrodynamics]]''' — time-varying fields; [[electromagnetic induction]], [[electromagnetic wave]]s
* '''[[Electromagnetic radiation]]''' — propagating oscillatory electric and magnetic fields; travel at the [[speed of light]]

== Electromagnetic potentials ==
Introducing the [[scalar potential]] <math>\phi</math> and [[vector potential]] <math>\mathbf{A}</math>:
:<math>\mathbf{E} = -\nabla\phi - \frac{\partial\mathbf{A}}{\partial t}</math>
:<math>\mathbf{B} = \nabla \times \mathbf{A}</math>

== Limit of validity ==
Classical electromagnetism breaks down at:
* Very small scales (~[[Compton wavelength]]) where [[quantum electrodynamics]] becomes necessary
* Extremely strong fields where [[nonlinear QED]] effects appear
* Atomic and subatomic scales where quantum effects dominate

== See also ==
{{Portal|Physics}}
* [[Electric field]]
* [[Electromagnetic wave]]
* [[Electromagnetism]]
* [[Magnetic field]]
* [[Maxwell's equations]]
* [[Quantum electrodynamics]]

== References ==
{{Reflist|30em}}

[[Category:Electromagnetism]]
[[Category:Classical physics]]
[[Category:Electrodynamics]]
